name: Government & Public Sector
description: Public sector AI governance including welfare administration, law enforcement, border control, and citizen services.

highRiskCategories.category: AI in law enforcement — individual risk assessment
highRiskCategories.description: AI systems intended to be used by law enforcement authorities or on their behalf to make individual risk assessments of natural persons in order to assess the risk of a natural person for offending or reoffending.

highRiskCategories.category: AI in migration and border control — polygraphs and risk assessment
highRiskCategories.description: AI systems intended to be used by competent public authorities or on their behalf as polygraphs and similar tools, or to assess a risk posed by a natural person entering the territory of a Member State.
AI systems intended to be used by a judicial authority or on their behalf to assist in researching and interpreting facts and the law and in applying the law to a concrete set of facts.

highRiskCategories.category: Prohibited AI practices in public sector
highRiskCategories.description: Social scoring by public authorities, real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces (with limited exceptions), and emotion recognition in workplace and education.

sectorRequirements.requirement: Conduct mandatory fundamental rights impact assessments (FRIA) before deploying high-risk AI systems in public administration, covering impacts on privacy, non-discrimination, freedom of expression, and access to services.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 27; OECD AI Principles; UNESCO AI Ethics; Canada AIDA proposed requirements

sectorRequirements.requirement: Ensure full prohibition compliance: no social scoring systems, no untargeted facial image scraping, no emotion inference in government services, and no predictive policing based solely on profiling.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 5; UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation; OECD AI Principles

sectorRequirements.requirement: Provide citizens with clear notification when they are interacting with or subject to AI systems in public services, including the right to request human review of automated decisions.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 50; GDPR Art. 22; Singapore MAIGF Stakeholder Interaction; Malaysia NGAIGE Transparency Principle

sectorRequirements.requirement: Register all high-risk AI systems deployed by public authorities in the EU AI database and maintain publicly accessible algorithmic registers for government AI use.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 49(3); UK Algorithmic Transparency Recording Standard; OECD AI Policy Recommendations

sectorRequirements.requirement: Implement robust access controls and security measures for AI systems processing sensitive government data, including classification-appropriate protections and supply chain security for AI vendors.
sectorRequirements.source: NIST AI RMF GOVERN and MANAGE functions; ISO 42001 Clause 8; Malaysia NGAIGE Safety & Security Principle

sectorRequirements.requirement: Ensure meaningful human oversight of AI systems used in law enforcement, judicial proceedings, and welfare eligibility determinations, with trained operators who can override AI outputs.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 14, Art. 26; NIST AI RMF MAP function; Canada AIDA proposed safeguards

sectorRequirements.requirement: Conduct inclusive stakeholder consultation including civil society, affected communities, and domain experts before deploying AI systems that significantly impact public services.
sectorRequirements.source: UNESCO AI Ethics Recommendation; OECD AI Principles; Malaysia NGAIGE Inclusiveness Principle; Singapore MAIGF

sectorRequirements.requirement: Establish independent external audit mechanisms for AI systems used in high-stakes government decisions, including periodic third-party assessments of accuracy, fairness, and compliance.
sectorRequirements.source: EU AI Act Art. 43; NIST AI RMF MEASURE function; UK AI Framework Accountability Principle

keyRisks.description: AI-driven benefit eligibility systems may systematically disadvantage vulnerable populations through biased risk profiling or opaque decision criteria.
AI surveillance and predictive systems in public spaces may deter lawful exercise of rights to assembly, protest, and expression.
Delegation of public decision-making to AI systems may undermine democratic accountability and due process requirements.
Dependence on proprietary AI systems from foreign vendors may compromise government data sovereignty and operational continuity.
AI systems used in judicial proceedings may produce anchoring effects, where judges over-rely on AI risk assessments in sentencing or bail decisions.
